lesson.title: Basis & Dimension
lesson.body: A basis for a vector space V is a set of vectors that is both linearly independent and spans V. The dimension of V is the number of vectors in any basis — this number is unique. The standard basis for Rⁿ consists of the unit vectors eᵢ (1 in position i, 0 elsewhere). The rank of a matrix equals the dimension of its column space, which equals the number of pivot positions in its row-reduced form.

rooms.question: What is the dimension of the standard vector space R⁴?
rooms.explanation: R⁴ has the standard basis {e₁, e₂, e₃, e₄} consisting of 4 vectors, so dim(R⁴) = 4.

rooms.question: Which of the following sets is a basis for R²?
steps: A basis must be linearly independent and span \mathbb{R}^2.
rooms.explanation: The standard basis {(1,0),(0,1)} is linearly independent and spans R². The second set is dependent, the third has too many vectors, and the fourth contains the zero vector.

rooms.question: The vectors v₁ = (1, 0, 1), v₂ = (0, 1, 0) form a basis for which space?
steps: These 2 vectors are linearly independent, so they span a 2-dimensional subspace of \mathbb{R}^3.
rooms.explanation: Two linearly independent vectors in R³ span a 2-dimensional subspace — a plane through the origin.

rooms.question: The coordinate vector of w = 3e₁ - 2e₂ + e₃ with respect to the standard basis of R³ is:
steps: Express w as a linear combination of e_1, e_2, e_3 and read off the coefficients.
rooms.explanation: The coordinates with respect to the standard basis are simply the components of w: (3, -2, 1).

rooms.question: Order the steps to determine if {v₁, v₂, v₃} is a basis for R³:
items: Form the matrix A = [v₁ | v₂ | v₃] as columns
Row reduce A to check for 3 pivot positions
Verify the set is linearly independent (no free variables)
Conclude it is a basis if and only if det(A) ≠ 0
rooms.explanation: First form the matrix of columns, row reduce to count pivots, verify independence (no free variables), then confirm basis status via nonzero determinant.

boss.name: The Dimension Wraith
boss.taunt: In infinite dimensions, your finite reasoning crumbles to dust!

questions.question: The set of all polynomials of degree at most 2 forms a vector space. What is its dimension?
questions.latex: P_2 = \{a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 x^2 : a_i \in \mathbb{R}\}
questions.explanation: P₂ has basis {1, x, x²} consisting of 3 elements, so dim(P₂) = 3.

questions.question: If a set of 4 vectors spans R³, can it be a basis for R³?
answers: No, a basis must have exactly dim(V) = 3 vectors
Yes, any spanning set is a basis
Yes, if the vectors are nonzero
No, because R³ cannot be spanned by 4 vectors
questions.explanation: A basis must be both linearly independent and spanning. A set of 4 vectors in R³ must be linearly dependent (since dim(R³) = 3), so it cannot be a basis.

questions.question: If V has dimension n and S = {v₁, ..., vₙ} is a linearly independent set in V, then:
answers: S is automatically a basis for V
S spans V but may not be a basis
S must be extended to a basis
S is not necessarily a basis
questions.explanation: In a finite-dimensional vector space, any linearly independent set with exactly dim(V) elements is automatically a basis — it must also span V.
